title: Giving an EC2 App AWS Access
description:
## Credentials on an EC2 Instance

A team is deploying an application onto **EC2 instances** in an Auto Scaling group. The application needs to read objects from an S3 bucket and publish metrics to CloudWatch via the AWS SDK. A developer proposes baking a long-lived IAM **user's access key and secret** into the application's configuration file so it can authenticate.

As the architect reviewing this, what is the correct and most secure way for the application to obtain AWS API permissions?

options.text: Create an IAM **role** with the required S3 and CloudWatch permissions and attach it to the instances via an **instance profile**; the SDK retrieves temporary, auto-rotating credentials from the instance metadata service.
Create one IAM user per instance, generate access keys for each, and store them in the application config so every instance has its own identity.
Add the application's account to an IAM **group** that has the S3 and CloudWatch policies attached, then log in with the group credentials.
Embed the root account access keys in the AMI so all instances launched from it inherit full access.

explanation: An **IAM role** attached through an **instance profile** is the standard, secure pattern for EC2. The role's permissions are exposed to the instance as **temporary credentials** delivered through the Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) and **automatically rotated** by AWS — nothing long-lived is ever stored on disk or in config, so there are no keys to leak or rotate manually. Option B (per-instance IAM users with embedded keys) reintroduces exactly the long-lived-credential problem you're trying to avoid and does not scale with an Auto Scaling group. Option C misunderstands IAM **groups**: groups are only a way to attach policies to a collection of *users* — they are not principals you can log in as and cannot be assumed by an instance. Option D (root keys baked into an AMI) is a severe security anti-pattern; root credentials should never be used programmatically and ideally never created at all.

hints.title: Prefer temporary over long-lived
hints.body: Anything that lives in a config file (an access key) can be exfiltrated. Roles hand out short-lived credentials that rotate automatically.

hints.title: What a group can and can't do
hints.body: An IAM group is just a policy-attachment container for users. It is not a principal an EC2 instance can use.
